aliases: Signature Chili Sauce with Jumbo Prawn

english: Signature Chili Sauce with Jumbo Prawn

food_subgroup: Crustaceans — cooked prawn dish with chilli sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for chilli sauce prawn specifically; closest parent is shrimp food product (FOODON_03311737).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers cooked crustaceans as the nearest defensible parent; no specific chilli prawn dish code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 covers prawn as a food substance; no specific prepared chilli prawn dish code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of chilli sauce jumbo prawn is approximately 350 g, comprising one or two large whole prawns with shell and head, coated in a generous amount of chilli-tomato sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli Prawn (cooked, with sauce) — adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Crustaceans, shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (NDB 15150) combined with chilli sauce component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'Signature Chilli Sauce Jumbo Prawn' was found. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo chilli prawn entries and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (NDB 15150) plus estimated sauce contribution (chilli-tomato sauce, egg, cornstarch). Per-100g values represent the composite dish including sauce. Confidence is medium due to adaptation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed seafood dishes with sauce — analogous estimate based on chilli crab and prawn dishes; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Signature Chilli Sauce Jumbo Prawn. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous chilli crab and prawn dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with moderate carbohydrate from sauce (cornstarch, tomato, sugar); low GI is consistent with high-protein seafood dishes. GL per serving = (35 × 5.8 × 3.5) / 100 = 7.1, rounded to 7.2. gi_category is 'low' per threshold ≤ 55.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1820 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium content from chilli sauce, soy-based condiments, and seasoning; a single restaurant serving may provide over 75% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ~35) but moderate carbohydrate load from sauce and added sugars
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of this dish is favourable for blood glucose management; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the sauce's added sugar content and moderate portion size to keep glycemic load within target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol (~145 mg per 100 g) and high sodium, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from prawn
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawn provides beneficial lean protein and some omega-3 fatty acids, but the high sodium and dietary cholesterol content of this dish warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Sauce Jumbo Prawn is a Singaporean-Chinese seafood dish featuring large whole prawns (typically tiger or black tiger prawns) cooked in a rich, tangy, and mildly spicy chilli-tomato sauce. The sauce is typically made from fresh red chillies, garlic, ginger, tomato paste or ketchup, egg, and cornstarch, producing a thick, glossy coating that clings to the prawn shells. The dish is wok-cooked at high heat, giving the prawns a slightly smoky char while the sauce caramelises around them. Texture is firm and succulent from the prawn meat, with a sticky, savoury-sweet sauce. It is closely related to the iconic Singapore Chilli Crab but uses jumbo prawns instead of crab. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of lean protein (~12.5 g per 100 g), selenium, vitamin B12, and phosphorus from the prawn, while the sauce contributes carbohydrates, sodium, and modest amounts of vitamin C and vitamin A from the chilli and tomato. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it relatively favourable for blood glucose management, though the high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is typically served as a shared main course at Chinese seafood restaurants in Singapore, accompanied by steamed white rice or deep-fried mantou (Chinese buns) for dipping in the sauce. The dish is halal-friendly when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or USDA entry for 'Signature Chilli Sauce Jumbo Prawn' as a composite restaurant dish was found. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo chilli prawn category data and USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp, with sauce component estimation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable chilli seafood dishes. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo chilli prawn entries and USDA SR Legacy NDB 15150 (cooked shrimp), with estimated sauce contribution. No single approved-source entry exactly matches this restaurant dish. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this dish type. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this dish type. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as prawn and vegetable-oil-based sauce contain negligible industrially produced trans fats. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' per threshold ≤ 55. GL per serving = (35 × 5.8 g carb × 350 g serving) / 100 = 7.2. 5. Sodium is high (~520 mg/100 g); a 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1820 mg sodium. 6. The dish is classified as Chinese-Singaporean cuisine; all five mandatory multilingual names (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are present. 7. Halal status is conditional on preparation without pork-derived ingredients; most Singapore seafood restaurants serving this dish are halal-certified or pork-free.
